162.reason: [The function parameter 'G' is passed by value, so modifications to the graph structure inside this function do not affect the original graph in main(). It should be passed by pointer (Graph *G).]

227.reason: [Because delete_vex receives G by value, the deletion has no effect on the original graph used in subsequent traversals.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.25 , reason:  [Because delete_vex receives G by value, the deletion has no effect on the original graph used in subsequent traversals.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.25 , reason:  [Because delete_vex receives G by value, the actual graph in main is not modified. Thus, the traversals after "deletion" still use the original graph.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.25 , reason:  [Because delete_vex receives G by value, the actual graph in main() remains unchanged. Thus, the traversals after this call still use the original graph including the vertex to be deleted.]

237.reason: [This line is not properly indented and only executes if the previous loop body is considered a single statement. The assignment to visited[delete_dot] is outside the loop but appears inside due to missing braces, causing logical error in BFS traversal setup.]

reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-1: 0.25 , reason:  [This line is not properly indented and is outside the loop due to missing braces. However, logically it's correct because it sets visited[delete_dot]=1 after resetting all. But the lack of braces makes the next line (BFSTraverse) not part of the intended block if more lines were added. Not a functional error here but poor style.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-2: 0.25 , reason:  [This line is not inside the for-loop due to missing braces. Only the last statement 'visited[delete_dot]=1;' is executed after the loop, but it's redundant since it's set again below. However, the real issue is that the graph wasn't actually modified.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-sc-3: 0.25 , reason:  [This line is not inside the for-loop due to missing braces. Only the last statement 'visited[delete_dot]=1;' is executed after the loop, but it's redundant since it's already set above. However, more critically, because the graph wasn't actually modified, marking visited[delete_dot]=1 is the only way deletion is simulated, but the adjacency lists still contain references to delete_dot, which may cause traversal to attempt visiting it if not properly skipped 鈥 but since visited[delete_dot] is set, it might appear to work. However, the real issue is that edges to delete_dot remain, potentially affecting neighbor ordering or causing undefined behavior if delete_dot were revisited via another path (though visited prevents that). The core error is the pass-by-value in delete_vex.]
